source_metadata.title_raw: Judge John S. Cooper 21 Stephens Way Berkeley, CA 94705 Dear Judge Cooper: Judge John S. Cooper Page 2 As a retired judge, you were assigned by the Judicial Council to the Alameda County Superior Court in 1989 for approximately two months.

content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 11, 1991

Judge John S. Cooper
21 Stephens Way
Berkeley, CA 94705

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-030

Dear Judge Cooper:

You have requested advice concerning your reporting
obligations under the conflict of interest disclosure provisions
of the Political Reform Act.1/

QUESTION

Are you required to file a Statement of Economic Interests
(Form 721) for 1990 since you were not assigned to a court at any
time during 1990?

CONCLUSION

Since you did not file a leaving office statement within 30
days of leaving your last assignment, you must file an annual
statement for 1990 no later than March 1, 1991.

1/ Government Code Section 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Judge John S. Cooper
Page 2

FACTS

As a retired judge, you were assigned by the Judicial Council
to the Alameda County Superior Court in 1989 for approximately two
months. You filed an annual Statement of Economic Interests (Form
721) on February 26, 1990, covering calendar year 1989. You were
not assigned to any court during 1990 and have not filed a
statement of economic interests for 1990.

ANALYSIS

Regulation 18724 (copy enclosed) states that court
commissioners and pro tem or retired judges are not required to
file statements of economic interests pursuant to Government Code
Section 87200, et seq., if they serve or expect to serve less than
30 days in any 12-month period.

However, once you expect to serve or you have served 30 days
or more in any 12-month period, you must begin filing statements
of economic interests with the clerk of the court to which you
have been assigned. You then must file annual statements until
your assignment ends. Once your assignment ends and you do not
receive a new assignment, you must file a leaving office statement
within 30 days of leaving your last assignment.

Since the last statement you filed was an annual statement
for 1989, you must now file either an annual statement for 1990,
or a combined annual and leaving office statement. If you decide
to file a combined statement, your leaving office date will be the
date you complete the form. The period covered will be January 1,
1990 through the date you complete the form. The statement should
be filed with the clerk of the Alameda County Superior Court,
which was your last assignment, no later than March 1, 1991. The
clerk of the court will make and retain a copy and forward the
original to the Commission. If you file the combined annual and
leaving office statement and you receive a new assignment in 1991
for which you expect to serve 30 days or more, you will be
required to complete an assuming office statement within 10 days
of your appointment.

However, if at this time you expect to serve more than 30
days in 1991, you may choose to file just the annual statement for
1990 covering the period January 1, 1990 through December 31,
1990. The annual statement must be filed on or before March 1,
1991, with the clerk of the Alameda County Superior Court. You
will continue filing annual statements with the clerk of the court
to which you have been assigned until you file a leaving office
statement.

Judge John S. Cooper
page 3


     If you have any questions or need further assistance, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel
                                        [illegible signature]
                                        Colleen McGee
                                        by: Colleen McGee
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Technical Assistance and
                                            Analysis Division

Enclosure

JOHN S. COOPER
JUDGE OF THE SUPERIOR COURT
(RETIRED)
21 STEPHENS WAY
BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA 94705

January 7, 1991

Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Statement of Economic Interests

Ladies and Gentlemen:

A few days ago, the office of the Alameda County Clerk
advised me that my name appeared on a list furnished them of
persons who might be required to file a Statement of
Interests for 1990. That office referred me to Lynn Martin
of the FPPC, to whom I indicated that it did not appear that
I was required to file a Form 721 at this time. She asked
that I write to you.

I served on the Oakland-Piedmont Municipal Court from
February 1, 1968, to December 29, 1970, and on the Superior
Court for Alameda County from that date until my retirement
on July 15, 1988. I timely filed all Annual Statements
required of me during that time, including the statement for
1987, filed on February 23, 1988. After retiring on July
15, 1988, I filed my Leaving Office Statement on July 18,
1988.

As a retired judge, I served on assignment by the
Chairperson of the Judicial Council for the balance of July
and for the months of August through November, 1988. I
filed a Form 721 covering that period, on March 1, 1989.

In 1989, I served on the Alameda County Superior Court
for approximately two months on assignment by the
Chairperson of the Judicial Council. I filed Form 721, by
reason of that service, on February 26, 1990.

I did not serve on assignment at any time in 1990.
I have filed Statements of Economic Interests for all
periods in which I did serve. Accordingly it does not
appear that I am required to file a Form 721 at this time.

Thank you for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

John S. Cooper

February --, 1991


Judge John S. Cooper
21 Stephens Way
Berkeley, CA 94705

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-030

Dear Judge Cooper:

     You have requested advice concerning your reporting
obligations under the conflict of interest disclosure provisions
of the Political Reform Act.1/

FACTS

     As a retired judge, you were assigned by the Judicial Council
to the Alameda County Superior Court in 1989 for approximately two
months. You filed an annual Statement of Economic Interests (Form
721) on February 26, 1990, covering calendar year 1989. You were
not assigned to any court during 1990 and have not filed a
statement of economic interests for 1990.

QUESTION

     Are you required to file a Statement of Economic Interests
(Form 721) for 1990 since you were not assigned to a court at any
time during 1990?




1/ Government Code Section 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Judge John Cooper
Page 2


CONCLUSION

     Since you did not file a leaving office statement within 30
days of leaving your last assignment, you must file an annual
statement for 1990 no later than March 1, 1991.

ANALYSIS

     Regulation 18724 (copy enclosed) states that court
commissioners and pro tem or retired judges are not required to
file statements of economic interests pursuant to Government Code
Section 87200, et seq., if they serve or expect to serve less than
30 days in any 12-month period.

     However, once you expect to serve or you have served 30 days
or more in any 12-month period, you must begin filing statements
of economic interests with the clerk of the court to which you
have been assigned. You then must file annual statements until
your assignment ends. Once your assignment ends and you do not
receive a new assignment, you must file a leaving office statement
within 30 days of leaving your last assignment.

     Since the last statement you filed was an annual statement
for 1989, you must now file either an annual statement for 1990,
or a combined annual and leaving office statement. If you decide
to file a combined statement, your leaving office date will be the
date you complete the form. The period covered will be January 1,
1990 through the date you complete the form. The statement should
be filed with the clerk of the court of Alameda Superior Court,
which was your last assignment, no later than March 1, 1991. The
clerk of the court will make and retain a copy and forward the
original to the Commission. If you file the combined annual and
leaving office statement and you receive a new assignment in 1991
for which you expect to serve 30 days or more, you will be
required to complete an assuming office statement within 10 days
of your appointment.

     However, if at this time you expect to serve more than 30
days in 1991, you may choose to file just the annual statement for
1990 covering the period January 1, 1990 through December 31,
1990. The annual statement must be filed on or before March 1,
1991, with the clerk of the court of Alameda Superior Court. You
will continue filing annual statements with the clerk of the court
to which you have been assigned until you file a leaving office
statement.

Judge John S. Cooper
page 3


     If you have any questions or need further assistance, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel


                                        by: Colleen McGee
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Technical Assistance and
                                            Analysis Division

Enclosure

sections.analysis:
Regulation 18724 (copy enclosed) states that court 
commissioners and pro tem or retired judges are not required to 
file statements of economic interests pursuant to Government Code 
Section 87200, et seq., if they serve or expect to serve less than 
30 days in any 12-month period. 
However, once you expect to serve or you have served 30 days 
or more in any 12-month period, you must begin filing statements 
of economic interests with the clerk of the court to which you 
have been assigned. 
You then must file annual statements until 
your assignment ends. 
Once your assignment ends and you do not 
receive a new assignment, you must file a leaving office statement 
within 30 days of leaving your last assignment. 
Since the last statement you filed was an annual statement 
for 1989, you must now file either an annual statement for 1990, 
or a combined annual and leaving office statement. 
If you decide 
to file a combined statement, your leaving office date will be the 
date you complete the form. 
The period covered will be January I, 
1990 through the date you complete the form. 
The statement should 
be filed with the clerk of the Alameda County Superior Court, 
which was your last assignment, no later than March I, 1991. 
The 
clerk of the court will make and retain a copy and forward the 
original to the Commission. 
If you file the combined annual and 
leaving office statement and you receive a new assignment in 1991 
for which you expect to serve 30 days or more, you will be 
required to complete an assuming office statement within 10 days 
of your appointment. 
However, if at this time you expect to serve more than 30 
days in 1991, you may choose to file just the annual statement for 
1990 covering the period January 1, 1990 through December 31, 
1990. 
The annual statement must be filed on or before March I, 
1991, with the clerk of the Alameda County Superior Court. 
You 
will continue filing annual statements with the clerk of the court 
to which you have been assigned until you file a leaving office 
statement. 


Judge John S. Cooper
